topic: Crypto - Hashing with SHA and BLAKE3

turns.content: Why does this even work this way where a streaming hash function like the blake3 crate can take an iterator but if I try to use it on a Vec it feels wrong? It seems like we should just write all bytes into memory before hashing instead of passing chunks through a trait.

That is a very common point of confusion when moving from sequential file processing to in-memory data. The blake3 crate actually implements the Hasher trait which allows you to feed it slices or iterators directly without ever needing an intermediate buffer, so technically you can pass a Vec directly but doing so defeats the purpose of streaming since you are already holding everything in RAM. The real benefit comes when you want to hash large files where reading the whole file into memory would cause a spike in your process size and potentially trigger swap usage or OOM errors on smaller machines.
Okay so I should use std::fs::File instead? But what if I need to verify integrity of multiple small files that fit in RAM and I want to avoid the overhead of creating a File handle for each one?

You can absolutely read into a Vec<u8> directly from a file using std::io::Read which gives you the same streaming behavior without needing to wrap it in a File object, so blake3 will still stream through that Vec just like it would with an iterator. The key insight is that blake3 doesn't care whether your input came from a File or a preloaded vector because its internal implementation uses a streaming algorithm that processes bytes as they become available rather than waiting for the entire buffer.

Wait so I can just call blake3_hash.update(&data) where data is my Vec<u8> and it works exactly like the file case? That clears up why we don't need to manually manage a hasher state across multiple reads anymore, right?

Exactly. You can pass any slice or collection that implements AsRef<[u8]> directly to blake3_hash.update() and it will handle the streaming internally without you needing to manually call finish() after every chunk. This is why Rust makes crypto crates so ergonomic compared to other languages where you often have to manage state explicitly, though if you really need to verify a massive file on disk you would still use std::fs::File combined with blake3_hash.update(&mut reader) to keep memory usage low.
